12	# Feature: DDx Agent Evaluation UX
13	
14	**ID:** FEAT-019
15	**Status:** In Progress
16	**Priority:** P1
17	**Owner:** DDx Team
18	
19	## Overview
20	
21	FEAT-019 owns the **evaluation UX layer**: comparison views, grading rubric
22	storage and display, and benchmark result aggregation in the web UI. It is a
23	child of FEAT-010 (exec substrate) — not a peer.
24	
25	Workflow shapes (comparison dispatch, replay, benchmark execution) live in the
26	skills library (`compare-prompts`, `replay-bead`, `benchmark-suite`). FEAT-019
27	provides the storage, display, and aggregation surfaces that those skills write
28	into and that users read from.
29	
30	This is deliberately an **evaluation and reporting layer** built on top of the
31	FEAT-010 run substrate and FEAT-006 agent service. The primary bead-execution
32	workflow is `ddx try` (FEAT-010), which runs an agent, captures evidence, and
33	lands or preserves the iteration. FEAT-019 adds: grading rubric management,
34	comparison record display, and benchmark result aggregation — it does not define
35	a competing foundational execution model or dispatch mechanism.
36	
37	### Ownership split
38	
39	- **FEAT-006/FEAT-010** own `ddx run`, `ddx try`, and `ddx work`, including
40	  the canonical bead execution workflow, `ComparisonRecord` persistence, and
41	  arm execution mechanics used by comparison skills.
42	- **FEAT-019** (this spec) owns **grading**, **replay**, and
43	  **benchmark suite execution** — the evaluation and reporting layer built
44	  on top of execute-bead preserved iterations and comparison dispatch.
45	- **FEAT-010** owns the exec substrate. FEAT-019 is a child of FEAT-010:
46	  comparison records, grading results, and benchmark aggregations are stored
47	  in the FEAT-010 run substrate. Workflow dispatch shapes (comparison,
48	  replay, benchmark) live in the skills library, not in DDx core.
49	- **FEAT-004** owns bead semantics including evidence. FEAT-019 adds
50	  evidence fields (session linkage) but does not modify bead close
51	  behavior.
52	
53	### Relationship to the run substrate (FEAT-010)
54	
55	FEAT-019 is a child of FEAT-010. Comparison, replay, and benchmark records
56	are stored in the FEAT-010 run substrate. FEAT-019 defines the storage schema
57	extensions (`type: comparison`, `type: replay`, `type: benchmark`) and owns
58	the web UI surfaces that display those records. It does not own worktree
59	management, run lifecycle, or dispatch mechanics.
60	
61	### Relationship to workflow skills and tools
62	
63	Workflow shapes live in the skills library:
64	- `compare-prompts` — N-arm dispatch + aggregation
65	- `replay-bead` — re-run with altered conditions + baseline diff
66	- `benchmark-suite` — compare across prompt matrix
67	
68	DDx owns grading invocation (`ddx agent grade`) and the evaluation UX.
69	Workflow plugins own the policies: when to experiment, which variables to
70	sweep, quality gates using grading results, automatic model selection, and
71	exploration loops.
72	
73	## Problem Statement
74	
75	**Current situation:**
76	- `ddx run` can dispatch one agent invocation and capture output/tokens/cost.
77	- Comparison skills can run multiple arms and check consensus (pass/fail).
78	- DDx Agent gives DDx full control of the agent loop with tool call logging.
79	- Beads define work. Commits capture verified results. But there is no
80	  structured way to compare harness outputs for the same prompt, replay a
81	  bead with a different model, or grade the quality of results.
82	
83	**Pain points:**
84	- When evaluating whether a local model (via DDx Agent) can replace a cloud
85	  model for a task class, there's no structured way to compare.
86	- Side-effecting agent runs (file writes, shell commands) can't be safely
87	  compared without isolation — running two agents in the same worktree
88	  produces interference.
89	- No way to answer "is this prompt good enough?" without manual review.
90	- Closed beads don't record which model/harness produced the result, so
91	  replaying with a different model requires manual prompt reconstruction.
92	
93	**Desired outcome:** Comparison, grading, and replay primitives that let
94	operators answer: "For this prompt, how does harness A compare to harness
95	B?" and "What if we reran this bead with a different model?" — with
96	concrete artifacts (diffs, outputs, grades) — automatically and repeatably.
97	
98	The canonical architecture is
99	[`SD-023`](../../02-design/solution-designs/SD-023-agent-evaluation.md).
100	
101	## Requirements
102	
103	### Functional
104	
105	> **Note on workflow shapes:** Comparison dispatch, replay, and benchmark
106	> execution are implemented as skills (`compare-prompts`, `replay-bead`,
107	> `benchmark-suite`) in the skills library. FEAT-019 owns the storage schema
108	> for their outputs and the web UI surfaces that display, aggregate, and manage
109	> those records. Requirements below describe the evaluation UX layer.
110	
111	**Comparison record storage and display**
112	1. Comparison records written by the `compare-prompts` skill (or any skill
113	   that produces a `ComparisonRecord`) are stored in the FEAT-010 run
114	   substrate with `type: comparison`.
115	2. The web UI exposes a comparison detail view: per-arm output, effect diff,
116	   tool-call summary, test results, and grades — all from the stored record.
117	3. Comparison list view shows recent comparisons with per-arm summary
118	   (tokens, cost, duration, pass/fail) sortable and filterable by date,
119	   harness, and bead.
120	4. `--format json` output for machine-readable consumption by dun checks
121	   and CI pipelines.
122	
123	**Side-effect capture storage**
124	5. Effect diffs (unified diff strings) and tool call log references are
125	   stored in the comparison record alongside text output.
126	6. The UI renders diffs with syntax highlighting; tool call logs are
127	   available as collapsible detail.
128	
129	**Grading rubric storage and display**
130	7. Grading rubrics are stored as named artifacts in the DDx artifact store.
131	   `ddx agent grade --rubric <file>` loads a rubric by path; named rubrics
132	   can be stored and referenced by name.
133	8. The web UI provides a rubric browser: list, view, and edit stored rubrics.
134	9. Grading results (score, max_score, pass, rationale per arm) are stored in
135	   the comparison record and displayed in the comparison detail view.
136	10. Custom grading rubric content is user/workflow-defined. DDx provides
137	    the storage, retrieval, and display mechanism.
138	
139	**Grading invocation and display**
140	11. `ddx agent grade --comparison <id>` sends a comparison record to a
141	    grading harness and records the evaluation. This is the DDx-owned
142	    grading invocation surface; dispatch mechanics reuse FEAT-006 harness
143	    registry.
144	12. `ddx agent grade --comparison <id> --grader claude` specifies which
145	    harness performs the grading (default: highest-preference available).
146	13. The grading prompt is a standardized template that presents:
147	    - The original task prompt
148	    - Each arm's text output
149	    - Each arm's effect diff
150	    - (Optional) test results
151	14. The grader returns a structured evaluation per arm stored in the
152	    comparison record:
153	    ```json
154	    {
155	      "arm": "agent",
156	      "score": 7,
157	      "max_score": 10,
158	      "pass": true,
159	      "rationale": "Correct implementation but missed edge case in..."
160	    }
161	    ```
162	15. Grades are appended to the comparison record in the FEAT-010 substrate.
163	16. Custom grading rubrics can be provided via `--rubric <file>` to
164	    replace the default template. DDx provides the rubric loading and
165	    storage mechanism; rubric content is user/workflow-defined (see
166	    requirements 7–10 above).
167	
168	**Benchmark result aggregation**
169	17. Benchmark run records (produced by the `benchmark-suite` skill) are
170	    stored in the FEAT-010 substrate with `type: benchmark`.
171	18. The web UI provides a benchmark results view: per-arm aggregate
172	    statistics (completed, failed, total tokens, cost, avg duration) and
173	    drill-down to individual comparison records per prompt.
174	19. `--format json` for machine-readable output (consumed by dun checks,
175	    workflow evaluation gates, CI pipelines).
176	19a. Historical benchmark results are retained and queryable; the UI
177	     supports trend views across benchmark runs of the same suite.
178	
179	**Bead-session linkage** (foundation for replay)
180	20. When a bead is closed after an agent run, record the `session_id` (from
181	    `sessions.jsonl`) and `closing_commit_sha` as bead evidence. The session
182	    already owns model, harness, tokens, and cost — do not duplicate those
183	    fields on the bead. `ddx bead show` resolves agent metadata from the
184	    linked session.
185	21. If no session link is recorded (manual close, legacy beads), replay
186	    still works but reports "baseline session unknown" and cannot provide
187	    a diff comparison.
188	
189	**Replay result display**
190	22. Replay execution is handled by the `replay-bead` skill. FEAT-019 owns
191	    the storage schema for replay records and the web UI display surface.
192	23. Replay records are stored in the FEAT-010 substrate with `type: replay`,
193	    linked to the originating bead via `bead_id` and `session_id`.
194	24. The web UI shows a replay detail view: the new diff, the baseline diff,
195	    a side-by-side comparison, and any `--post-run` test result.
196	25. The bead detail view in the web UI includes a "Replays" tab listing all
197	    replay records for that bead with model/harness/outcome summary.
198	
199	### Non-Functional
200	
201	- **Storage:** Comparison records include diffs and outputs which can be
202	  large. Use the same attachment-backed storage as session logs (FEAT-006).
203	- **Query performance:** Comparison list and benchmark aggregation views must
204	  load without full record deserialization — store per-arm summary fields
205	  at the record envelope level.
206	- **Format compatibility:** `--format json` output must be stable for CI
207	  consumers; schema changes must be additive.
208	
209	## Design Principles
210	
211	### Evaluation UX, not execution framework
212	
213	FEAT-019 provides the storage schema and UI surfaces for evaluation results:
214	- Store and display comparison records (written by skills)
215	- Store, manage, and display grading rubrics
216	- Aggregate and display benchmark results
217	- Invoke the grading harness and record results
218	
219	FEAT-019 does **not** provide:
220	- Comparison dispatch (→ `compare-prompts` skill)
221	- Replay execution (→ `replay-bead` skill)
222	- Benchmark execution (→ `benchmark-suite` skill)
223	- Prompt optimization strategies
224	- Model selection policies
225	- Grading rubric content (beyond a sensible default)
226	
227	Skills compose DDx primitives into workflow shapes. FEAT-019 stores and
228	surfaces what skills produce.
229	
230	## CLI Interface
231	
232	> **Workflow dispatch shapes** (`compare`, `replay`, `benchmark`) are skills
233	> in the skills library. FEAT-019 owns the grading invocation surface and the
234	> record query/display surface.
235	
236	```bash
237	# Grade a comparison using claude as grader
238	ddx agent grade --comparison cmp-abc123 --grader claude
239	
240	# Grade with custom rubric
241	ddx agent grade --comparison cmp-abc123 --rubric rubrics/code-quality.md
242	
243	# List recent comparisons
244	ddx agent compare --list
245	
246	# Show comparison detail
247	ddx agent compare --show cmp-abc123 --format json
248	
249	# Workflow dispatch shapes are skills — not DDx CLI core:
250	# compare-prompts skill:  N-arm dispatch + aggregation
251	# replay-bead skill:      re-run with altered conditions + baseline diff
252	# benchmark-suite skill:  compare across prompt matrix
253	```
254	
255	### Configuration
256	
257	```yaml
258	# .ddx/config.yaml
259	agent:
260	  compare:
261	    default_grader: claude         # harness to use for grading
262	```
263	
264	## User Stories
265	
266	### US-190: Developer Views Comparison Results in Web UI
267	**As a** developer evaluating whether a local model can handle a task class
268	**I want** to view comparison records in the web UI after the `compare-prompts` skill runs
269	**So that** I can see concrete differences in output quality and cost
270	
271	**Acceptance Criteria:**
272	- Given a comparison record exists in the FEAT-010 substrate, then the web UI
273	  comparison list shows per-arm summary (tokens, cost, duration)
274	- Given I open a comparison detail view, then I see per-arm output, effect diff,
275	  and grades (if graded)
276	- Given `--format json`, then machine-readable comparison output is available
277	  via CLI for CI consumption
278	
279	### US-191: Developer Grades Agent Outputs
280	**As a** developer assessing prompt quality
281	**I want** to have a smart model grade the outputs of a comparison run
282	**So that** I get a structured quality score without manual review
283	
284	**Acceptance Criteria:**
285	- Given a completed comparison, when I run `ddx agent grade --comparison <id>`,
286	  then a grading harness evaluates each arm
287	- Given the grading completes, then each arm has a score, pass/fail, and
288	  rationale in the comparison record
289	- Given I specify `--rubric custom.md`, then the custom rubric replaces
290	  the default grading template
291	
292	### US-192: CI Pipeline Runs Prompt Regression Test
293	**As a** CI system ensuring prompt quality
294	**I want** to compare agent outputs against a recorded baseline
295	**So that** prompt changes that degrade quality are caught before merge
296	
297	**Acceptance Criteria:**
298	- Given a comparison with an `agent` arm and the virtual provider baseline,
299	  when the DDx Agent output diverges significantly, then the
300	  comparison record reflects the difference
301	- Given `--format json` output, then CI can parse the comparison and
302	  fail the pipeline on grade regression
303	
304	### US-193: Developer Inspects Effect Diffs in Comparison View
305	**As a** developer reviewing comparison experiment results
306	**I want** to see each arm's effect diff in the comparison detail view
307	**So that** I can understand what each model actually changed in the codebase
308	
309	**Acceptance Criteria:**
310	- Given a comparison record with effect diffs, then the web UI comparison
311	  detail view renders each arm's diff with syntax highlighting
312	- Given a comparison has tool call logs, then they are accessible as
313	  collapsible detail in the UI
314	- Given an arm failed, then its failure reason is shown in the comparison view
315	
316	### US-194: Developer Views Replay Results on Bead Detail
317	**As a** developer evaluating local model capability
318	**I want** to view replay results in the bead detail view after the `replay-bead` skill runs
319	**So that** I can compare the new model's output against the known-good result
320	
321	**Acceptance Criteria:**
322	- Given a replay record exists for a bead, then the bead detail view includes
323	  a "Replays" tab listing all replays with model/harness/outcome summary
324	- Given I open a replay detail view, then I see the new diff, the baseline diff,
325	  and a side-by-side comparison
326	- Given a replay has a `--post-run` test result, then pass/fail is shown in the
327	  replay detail view
328	
329	### US-195: Bead Links to Agent Session on Close
330	**As a** developer building a replay corpus
331	**I want** bead close to record the session ID and closing commit
332	**So that** future replays can reconstruct the exact prompt and baseline
333	
334	**Acceptance Criteria:**
335	- Given an agent run closes bead ddx-xxx, then `session_id` and
336	  `closing_commit_sha` are recorded as bead evidence
337	- Given `ddx bead show ddx-xxx`, then model/harness/tokens/cost are
338	  resolved from the linked session (not stored on the bead)
339	- Given a bead closed without an agent run, then no session link exists
340	  and `ddx bead show` omits agent metadata
341	
342	### US-196: Evaluation UI Displays Records from Preserved Try Iterations
343	**As** a developer evaluating bead execution quality
344	**I want** FEAT-019 UI to display comparison/grading records sourced from preserved `ddx try` iterations
345	**So that** the evaluation UX surfaces what skills produce from preserved iterations — not a parallel execution model
346	
347	**Acceptance Criteria:**
348	- Given preserved `ddx try` iterations exist for a bead, when skills write
349	  comparison or benchmark records referencing those iterations, then FEAT-019
350	  UI displays them without requiring a separate evaluation execution path.
351	- Given an evaluation record references a preserved try iteration, when
352	  provenance is inspected in the UI, then it traces back to the originating
353	  try session ID and hidden ref — no FEAT-019-owned duplicate provenance record exists.
354	
355	## Dependencies
356	
357	- FEAT-010 (Executions) — run substrate; comparison/replay/benchmark records
358	  are stored here; FEAT-019 is a child of FEAT-010
359	- FEAT-006 (Agent Service) — harness registry, session logging, grading dispatch
360	- FEAT-008 (Web UI) — UI rendering surfaces for comparison/replay/benchmark views
361	- FEAT-014 (Token Awareness) — token/cost tracking per arm (displayed in comparison views)
362	- FEAT-012 (Git Awareness) — git diff rendering in comparison views
363	- Skills library — `compare-prompts`, `replay-bead`, `benchmark-suite` write the
364	  records that FEAT-019 displays
365	
366	## Out of Scope
367	
368	- **Comparison dispatch** — running N harnesses on the same prompt is the
369	  `compare-prompts` skill, not DDx core.
370	- **Replay execution** — running a bead with a different model is the
371	  `replay-bead` skill, not DDx core.
372	- **Benchmark execution** — running a prompt matrix is the `benchmark-suite`
373	  skill, not DDx core.
374	- **Exploration loops** — "try 10 ideas to improve metric X" is a workflow
375	  plugin loop that calls skills, not a DDx execution mode.
376	- **Model selection policies** — workflow tools decide which model for which
377	  task based on comparison results. DDx provides the data.
378	- **Prompt optimization** — automatic prompt rewriting is out of scope.
379	  DDx provides comparison and grading; plugins provide the iteration loop.
380	- **Structured git trailers** — bead + session log already have the data.
381	  Don't duplicate into git commit metadata.
382	- **Autonomy semantics and exploration policy** — DDx does not define what autonomy levels mean or when to run experiments; those are delegated to workflow tools
383	- **Escalation and supervisory rules** — whether to accept a result, retry, or escalate is workflow tool policy; DDx provides the comparison and grading data
384	- Container or VM-level sandboxing
385	- Cross-repo or cross-project comparison
386

390	`docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-019-agent-evaluation.md`.

10	# Feature: Multi-Node Dashboard UI
11	
12	**ID:** FEAT-021
13	**Status:** In Progress
14	**Priority:** P1
15	**Owner:** DDx Team
16	
17	## Overview
18	
19	Extends the FEAT-008 web UI with a node/project-aware dashboard. The UI can
20	show a combined view of beads and DDx run activity across all registered
21	projects on a node, or narrow to a specific project for context-dependent
22	views (artifact browser, dependency graph, run history, commit log). Node,
23	project, and page are all embedded in the URL so every view is bookmarkable
24	and shareable.
25	
26	The dashboard UI is implemented as a SvelteKit application with graphql-request for
27	GraphQL data fetching. Every page uses `+page.ts` load functions to fetch data
28	from the `/graphql` endpoint defined in SD-022.
29	
30	## Problem Statement
31	
32	**Current situation:** The FEAT-008 UI is implicitly scoped to the single
33	project the server was started in. There is no node concept, no project picker,
34	and no combined cross-project view for beads or layer-aware run history.
35	
36	**Pain points:**
37	- Operator cannot see all active work across projects in one dashboard
38	- Navigating to a different project requires restarting the server in that
39	  project's directory
40	- Deep links to a specific bead, artifact, or run record are not stable because the URL
41	  carries no project context
42	- Queue-drain evidence is flattened as agent sessions instead of preserving
43	  the `work` -> `try` -> `run` hierarchy from FEAT-010
44	- Node identity is invisible in the UI — there is no indication of which
45	  machine or server instance is being viewed
46	
47	**Desired outcome:** A single `ddx server` UI that surfaces everything
48	happening on the node. The operator can scan the full bead queue and run
49	history across all projects, drill into a specific project for artifact-level
50	work, and bookmark any view with a stable URL.
51	
52	## URL Structure
53	
54	All routes embed context so every view is directly addressable:
55	
56	```
57	/                                     → redirect to /nodes/:nodeId
58	/federation                           → federation overview (hub-mode only); see FEAT-026
59	/nodes/:nodeId                        → node overview (health, project list)
60	/nodes/:nodeId/runs                   → combined run history (all projects)
61	/nodes/:nodeId/beads                  → combined bead queue (all projects)
62	/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId                   → project overview
63	/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/beads             → project beads
64	/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/artifacts         → artifact browser
65	/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/documents         → document browser
66	/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/graph             → doc dependency graph
67	/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/commits           → commit log
68	/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/runs              → project run history
69	/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/runs/:runId       → project run detail
70	```
71	
72	**Combined views** (`/nodes/:nodeId/runs`, `/nodes/:nodeId/beads`) operate
73	across all registered projects. They call node-scoped read APIs for beads and
74	the unified run substrate (since one server = one node). When the node is a
75	**federation hub** (FEAT-026, ADR-007), these combined views accept
76	`?scope=federation` to fan out across registered spokes; the default scope
77	remains node-local. The hub also serves `/nodes/:spokeId/...` for any
78	registered spoke, resolving the route through the federated read fan-out.
79	
80	**Project-scoped views** (`/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/...`) pass the
81	project ID to the existing `/api/projects/:project/...` API routes defined in
82	SD-019. The UI must always resolve which project context to bind before
83	making API calls.
84	
85	The `:nodeId` segment is the stable node ID from `GET /api/node` (e.g.
86	`node-7029e8d6`). The `:projectId` segment is the stable project ID from
87	`GET /api/projects` (e.g. `proj-96d7ea83`).
88	
89	## Views

93	- Node name, ID, and uptime
94	- Health summary cards: library status, bead store, doc graph per project
95	- Project list with last-seen timestamp and health badge
96	- Quick links to combined runs and combined beads
97	- Server version and started_at

99	**GraphQL Query:** `node` query from FEAT-020, `projects` query for project list.

210	### Project Run Detail (`/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/runs/:runId`)
211	
212	Layer-aware run detail for a single `work`, `try`, or `run` record. Work
213	records show child try attempts, try records show child layer-1 runs, and
214	layer-1 runs show prompt/config summary, selected harness/provider/model,
215	power bounds, cost/token/duration signals, evidence links, and produced
216	artifact links.
217	
218	**GraphQL Query:** `run` by id with project filter per FEAT-010.
219	
220	**File:** `src/routes/nodes/[nodeId]/projects/[projectId]/runs/[runId]/+page.svelte`
221	
222	## Navigation
223	
224	The global navigation bar shows:
225	- Node name + ID (links to node overview)
226	- Project picker dropdown (populated from `/api/projects`)
227	  - Selecting a project navigates to `/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId`
228	  - "All projects" option navigates to combined views
229	- Active page tab: Beads | Artifacts | Graph | Runs | Commits
230	
231	The project picker changes the `:projectId` segment in-place while preserving
232	the current page tab. So switching project while on the Graph tab navigates to
233	the new project's graph directly. When switching from a run detail to another
234	project, the UI falls back to that project's run history unless the same run id
235	exists in the target project.
236	
237	**GraphQL Query:** `projects` for picker options.
238	
239	**File:** `src/lib/components/ProjectPicker.svelte`
240	
241	## Requirements
242	
243	### Functional
244	
245	1. Root `/` redirects to `/nodes/:nodeId` using the ID from `GET /api/node`.
246	2. All URLs listed in the URL Structure section are valid deep links — loading
247	   them directly in a browser renders the correct view with data.
248	3. The node overview page is populated from `GET /api/node` and
249	   `GET /api/projects`.
250	4. Combined bead view fetches all projects' beads client-side and merges them.
251	5. Combined run view fetches node-wide run records from the unified run
252	   substrate and preserves `work`, `try`, and `run` layer labels.
253	6. Project-scoped views bind all API calls to
254	   `/api/projects/:project/...` per SD-019.
255	7. The project picker is present on every project-scoped page and updates the
256	   URL when changed.
257	8. A new `GET /api/projects/:project/commits` endpoint serves git commit
258	   history for the project.
259	9. Commit log view links commits to matching beads where
260	   `closing_commit_sha` matches.
261	10. All filter/sort/search state is stored in URL query parameters so filtered
262	    views are also bookmarkable.
263	11. Project run detail routes support parent/child drill-down:
264	    `work` -> `try` -> `run`, with breadcrumbs back up the hierarchy.
265	12. Project artifact routes expose FEAT-008 media-type rendering and link to
266	    producing runs when `generated_by` is present.
267	
268	### Non-Functional
269	
270	- Combined bead view with 500 beads across 5 projects renders in <500ms
271	  (client-side merge).
272	- Navigation between views within the same node+project uses client-side
273	  routing (no full page reload).
274	- The UI degrades gracefully when a project becomes unavailable: its beads and
275	  run records are shown with a stale badge rather than being silently dropped.
276	
277	## User Stories
278	
279	### US-090: Operator Views All Active Work in One Dashboard
280	**As an** operator managing multiple projects on one machine
281	**I want** to see all open beads and recent run records from every project
282	**So that** I can understand the full workload without switching context
283	
284	**Acceptance Criteria:**
285	- Given multiple projects are registered, when I open `/nodes/:nodeId/beads`,
286	  then I see beads from all projects with a project badge on each
287	- Given I filter by project, then only beads from that project are shown and
288	  the URL updates to reflect the filter
289	- Given I open `/nodes/:nodeId/runs`, then I see run records from all projects
290	  merged and sorted by time with a project badge and a layer badge (`work`,
291	  `try`, or `run`) on each record
292	- Given I click a run record, then I navigate to that run's project-scoped
293	  detail at `/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/runs/:runId`; the `projectId`
294	  is carried in each run record so the combined list can form this URL without
295	  a separate lookup; I see layer-appropriate detail: queue inputs for `work`,
296	  bead/worktree fields for `try`, execution summary for `run`
297	- Given I navigate back from a run detail, then the combined run list is
298	  restored with the same sort and filter state
299	
300	**E2E Test:** `node-beads.spec.ts` and `node-runs.spec.ts` — full workflows:
301	- node-beads: open combined beads → verify cross-project beads with project badges → filter by project → verify URL and filtered results → click bead → verify navigation to project-scoped bead detail → press Back
302	- node-runs: open combined runs → verify cross-project records with layer and project badges → filter by layer → click work record → verify detail → click child try → verify fields → press Back through hierarchy
303	
304	### US-091: Operator Navigates to a Project-Scoped View
305	**As an** operator investigating one project
306	**I want** to select a project and see its artifacts, graph, runs, and commits
307	**So that** I can do project-specific work without losing the node context
308	
309	**Acceptance Criteria:**
310	- Given I am on the node overview, when I click a project, then I navigate to
311	  that project's overview at `/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId`
312	- Given I am on the artifact browser for project A, when I switch projects in
313	  the picker, then I navigate to the artifact browser for project B without
314	  losing the current page context (e.g. still on `/artifacts`)
315	- Given I copy the current URL and open it in a new tab, then I see the same
316	  view with the same project and page selected
317	- Given I switch projects while on a run detail, then the UI falls back to
318	  that project's run history
319	
320	**E2E Test:** `navigation.spec.ts` — full workflow: open node overview → click project → verify project overview URL → open artifact browser → switch project in picker → verify URL and content updated to project B → copy URL → open in new tab → verify same view → navigate to run detail → switch project → verify fallback to run history
321	
322	### US-092: Operator Bookmarks a Filtered View
323	**As an** operator who checks the ready queue every morning
324	**I want** to bookmark the ready queue filtered to my project
325	**So that** I can open it directly without navigating through the UI
326	
327	**Acceptance Criteria:**
328	- Given I filter the bead list to status=open and project=ddx, then the URL
329	  contains those filter parameters
330	- Given I reload the page with that URL, then the same filter is applied
331	  without any interaction
332	- Given I share the URL with a colleague on the same machine, then they see
333	  the same view
334	
335	**E2E Test:** `navigation.spec.ts` — full workflow: apply status + project filters → verify URL params → copy URL → reload page → verify same filter applied without interaction
336	
337	### US-093: Operator Traces a Bead to Its Closing Commit
338	**As an** operator reviewing completed work
339	**I want** to see the git commit that closed a bead
340	**So that** I can inspect the code change that fulfilled the bead's acceptance
341	
342	**Acceptance Criteria:**
343	- Given a bead has `closing_commit_sha` set, when I view the commit log, then
344	  that commit is highlighted and linked to the bead
345	- Given I click the commit link from the bead detail, then I navigate to the
346	  commit log with that commit in view
347	- Given I click the bead link from the commit row, then I navigate to that
348	  bead's detail
349	
350	**E2E Test:** `commits.spec.ts` — full workflow: open bead with closing commit → click commit link → verify commit log opens with commit highlighted → click bead link from commit row → verify bead detail opens → press Back → verify commit log restored at same position
351	
352	### US-094: Operator Sees Node Identity in the UI
353	**As an** operator with multiple machines
354	**I want** to see which node I am connected to
355	**So that** I don't confuse the eitri server with the bragi server
356	
357	**Acceptance Criteria:**
358	- Given I open the web UI, then the node name and ID are visible in the
359	  navigation bar or node overview
360	- Given the URL contains `:nodeId`, then it matches the ID returned by
361	  `GET /api/node`
362	
363	**E2E Test:** `navigation.spec.ts` — full workflow: open UI → verify node name and ID visible in nav → verify URL nodeId matches GET /api/node response
364	
365	### US-094b: Operator Opens Layer-Aware Run Routes
366	**As an** operator reviewing DDx execution evidence
367	**I want** project-scoped run routes for history and detail
368	**So that** queue drains, bead attempts, and single agent invocations remain
369	  distinct in the URL and UI
370	
371	**Acceptance Criteria:**
372	- Given I navigate to `/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/runs`, then I see
373	  project-scoped run records with filter chips for layer, status, bead,
374	  harness, and time range; active filters are encoded in the URL
375	- Given I click a `work` record, then I navigate to
376	  `/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/runs/:runId` and see the work detail:
377	  queue inputs, selected beads, stop condition, and child try attempts
378	- Given I click a child `try`, then I see bead id, base/result revisions,
379	  worktree path, merge/preserve result, checks, and child layer-1 runs
380	- Given I click a layer-1 `run`, then I see prompt/config summary, power
381	  bounds, selected harness/provider/model, tokens, cost, duration, output, and
382	  evidence links
383	- Given I am viewing any run detail, then breadcrumbs show the full
384	  `work → try → run` path and each crumb is a navigable link
385	- Given a run produced an artifact, then the detail route links to the artifact
386	  browser entry and that artifact links back to the producing run
387	
388	**E2E Test:** `runs.spec.ts` — full workflow: navigate to project runs → apply layer filter → verify URL → click work record → verify work detail fields → click child try → verify try fields → click layer-1 run → verify all fields → follow evidence link → press Back through breadcrumbs to run list → verify filter state restored → follow artifact link from run → verify artifact links back to run
389	
390	## New API Required
391	
392	In addition to the endpoints delivered by FEAT-020 and FEAT-010, FEAT-021
393	requires:
394	
395	| Endpoint | Description |
396	|----------|-------------|
397	| `GET /api/projects/:project/commits` | Git log for the project; supports `?limit=&since=&author=` |
398	
399	All other data is served by endpoints already defined in FEAT-002, FEAT-004,
400	FEAT-006, FEAT-008, FEAT-010, and SD-019.
401	
402	## Implementation Notes
403	
404	### Router
405	
406	SvelteKit handles routing automatically based on `src/routes/` file structure.
407	Routes are nested using the folder hierarchy:
408	
409	```
410	src/routes/
411	├── +layout.svelte              → Node+Project layout with nav
412	├── +page.ts                    → Root redirect to /nodes/:nodeId
413	└── nodes/
414	    └── [$nodeId]/
415	        ├── +layout.ts          → Load node context
416	        ├── +page.svelte        → NodeOverview
417	        ├── beads/              → CombinedBeads
418	        │   └── +page.svelte
419	        ├── runs/               → CombinedRuns
420	        │   └── +page.svelte
421	        └── projects/
422	            └── [$projectId]/
423	                ├── +page.svelte      → ProjectOverview
424	                ├── beads/            → ProjectBeads
425	                │   └── +page.svelte
426	                ├── artifacts/        → ArtifactBrowser
427	                │   └── +page.svelte
428	                ├── documents/        → DocumentBrowser
429	                │   └── +page.svelte
430	                ├── graph/            → DocGraph
431	                │   └── +page.svelte
432	                ├── commits/          → CommitLog
433	                │   └── +page.svelte
434	                └── runs/             → ProjectRuns
435	                    ├── +page.svelte
436	                    └── [$runId]/
437	                        └── +page.svelte
438	```
439	
440	The `[$nodeId]` and `[$projectId]` dynamic route segments capture URL parameters.
441	SvelteKit's `+layout.ts` and `+page.ts` files define load functions that
442	fetch data via graphql-request.
443	
444	### Data Layer
445	
446	graphql-request is the GraphQL client for data fetching, with graphql-ws for
447	subscriptions. Pages define queries inline using the `gql` tagged template
448	and manually typed response interfaces in `+page.ts` load functions.
449	
450	Example bead list query:
451	
452	```typescript
453	// src/routes/nodes/$nodeId/beads/+page.ts
454	import { gql } from 'graphql-request';
455	import { createClient } from '$lib/gql/client';
456	
457	const BEAD_LIST_QUERY = gql`
458	  query BeadList($projectID: ID) {
459	    beads(projectID: $projectID, first: 50) {
460	      edges {
461	        node {
462	          id
463	          title
464	          status
465	          priority
466	          projectID
467	        }
468	      }
469	    }
470	  }
471	`;
472	
473	export const load = async ({ fetch, params }) => {
474	  const client = createClient(fetch);
475	  const data = await client.request(BEAD_LIST_QUERY, { projectID: params.projectId });
476	  return { beads: data.beads };
477	};
478	```
479	
480	graphql-ws subscription support enables real-time updates without polling.
481	
482	Example project run query:
483	
484	```typescript
485	// src/routes/nodes/$nodeId/projects/$projectId/runs/+page.ts
486	import { gql } from 'graphql-request';
487	import { createClient } from '$lib/gql/client';
488	
489	const RUN_LIST_QUERY = gql`
490	  query ProjectRuns($projectID: ID!, $layer: RunLayer) {
491	    runs(projectID: $projectID, layer: $layer, first: 50) {
492	      edges {
493	        node {
494	          id
495	          layer
496	          status
497	          beadID
498	          artifactID
499	          parentRunID
500	          startedAt
501	          completedAt
502	        }
503	      }
504	    }
505	  }
506	`;
507	
508	export const load = async ({ fetch, params, url }) => {
509	  const client = createClient(fetch);
510	  const layer = url.searchParams.get('layer');
511	  const data = await client.request(RUN_LIST_QUERY, {
512	    projectID: params.projectId,
513	    layer
514	  });
515	  return { runs: data.runs };
516	};
517	```
518	
519	### Project Context
520	
521	The `ProjectStore` (using Svelte 5 runes) stores the selected node ID and
522	project ID. It's populated from URL params by `+layout.ts` load functions:
523	
524	```typescript
525	// src/lib/stores/project.ts
526	import { writable } from 'svelte/store';
527	
528	export const projectStore = writable<{
529	  nodeId: string;
530	  projectId?: string;
531	} | null>(null);
532	```
533	
534	Components read from `projectStore` to construct API URLs and filter queries.
535	
536	### Testing
537	
538	Playwright e2e tests verify the complete end-to-end workflow for each user
539	story. Each spec covers the full flow: entry point → actions → outcome →
540	navigation back. Tests use real ddx-server with seeded fixture data; no
541	mocking of the GraphQL or HTTP layer.
542	
543	| E2E Spec | User Stories | Workflow Covered |
544	|---|---|---|
545	| `navigation.spec.ts` | US-091, US-092, US-094 | Root redirect → node overview → project picker → project switch preserving page context → bookmarkable URL round-trip → node identity visible |
546	| `node-beads.spec.ts` | US-090 (beads) | Combined bead view → cross-project badges → filter by project → click bead → navigate to project-scoped detail → Back |
547	| `node-runs.spec.ts` | US-090 (runs) | Combined run history → cross-project layer badges → filter → drill work→try→run → breadcrumbs back to list |
548	| `runs.spec.ts` | US-094b | Project runs → apply layer filter → work detail → try detail → run detail → evidence link → artifact link → Back through full hierarchy |
549	| `commits.spec.ts` | US-093 | Bead with closing commit → commit log → highlighted commit → bead cross-link → Back |
550	
551	## Dependencies
552	
553	- FEAT-008 (web UI foundation — build pipeline, embed pattern, existing views)
554	- FEAT-020 (node identity and project registry — required for /api/node and /api/projects)
555	- SD-019 (project-scoped API routing — /api/projects/:project/...)
556	- FEAT-004 (beads)
557	- FEAT-006 (agent service metadata and native session references)
558	- FEAT-007 (doc graph)
559	- FEAT-010 (unified run substrate and run read APIs)
560	- FEAT-012 (git awareness — for commit log endpoint)
561	
562	## Federation Compatibility Hooks (FEAT-026)
563	
564	When the node runs as a **federation hub** (FEAT-026, ADR-007), the UI gains:
565	
566	- `/federation` — federation overview route showing the hub, its registered
567	  spokes, each spoke's liveness (`live` / `stale` / `offline`), and
568	  compatibility status (`live` / `degraded` / `rejected`). Owned by FEAT-026.
569	- `/nodes/:nodeId/...` resolves across the federation: when `:nodeId` is a
570	  registered spoke, the hub serves the route from the spoke's federated read
571	  surface. Spoke UIs remain directly reachable as a fallback.
572	- Combined views (`/nodes/:nodeId/beads`, `/nodes/:nodeId/runs`) accept the
573	  `?scope=federation` query parameter; when set on a hub, the view fans out
574	  across all spokes and tags each row with `node_id`, `project_id`, and a
575	  `node` badge in addition to the existing project badge.
576	- Rows sourced from a `stale`, `offline`, or `degraded` spoke carry the
577	  matching badge; the page does not fail when a spoke is unreachable.
578	
579	The naming convention from ADR-007 applies to UI copy: prefer
580	**federation / hub / spoke** over `coordinator`, `primary`, `replica`.
581	
582	FEAT-021 contributes only the URL grammar and route stubs above; the
583	federation registry, hub endpoints, fan-out, version handshake, and
584	`?scope=federation` data plumbing are owned by FEAT-026.
585	
586	## Out of Scope
587	
588	- Federation registry, hub/spoke HTTP endpoints, fan-out client, federated
589	  GraphQL fields, and version handshake. Owned by FEAT-026 and ADR-007.
590	  FEAT-021 contributes only the route grammar (`/federation`,
591	  hub-resolved `/nodes/:nodeId/...`, `?scope=federation`).
592	- Cross-machine writes (federation is read-only aggregation in v1; see
593	  ADR-007 write-routing contract for Story 15).
594	- Authentication beyond ts-net (ADR-006).
595	- Real-time push (polling is sufficient for v1).
596	- Mobile layout.

Re-queue, exposed in the web UI on layer=`try` and layer=`run` Runs rows
(FEAT-008 US-086c), is the **manual operator counterpart** to the
evaluation auto-retry policies skills compose on top of FEAT-019 records.

`run_requeue` audit event on the originating bead (schema in FEAT-010

input.new_string:
| `ddx_artifact_regenerate` | `POST /api/artifacts/:id/regenerate` | Trigger regeneration of one artifact; returns the new run id | **write — narrow** |
| (GraphQL) | `Mutation.runRequeue` | Re-queue the originating bead of an existing run record (manual operator re-queue from the Runs UI); requires `idempotencyKey`; emits a `run_requeue` audit event on the bead | **write — narrow** |

The HTTP/MCP read surface is total over the unified run substrate.

The write surfaces added by this feature are limited to
`artifactRegenerate` and `runRequeue`. All other run invocation
(layer-1 ad-hoc, layer-2 bead attempts, layer-3 queue drain) remains
CLI-only. Additional write endpoints require a separate feature update;
they are not implicit in this read-coverage expansion.

### Re-queue audit events

The `runRequeue` mutation reopens the originating bead and appends a
`run_requeue` event to that bead's audit log. Concurrent or repeat
submissions with the same `idempotencyKey` collapse to a single
re-queue and a single event (`deduplicated=true` is returned to all
subsequent callers); if the cached idempotency record points at a
missing bead, the requeue is replayed against the run's current
originating bead. The event uses the standard `bead.BeadEvent` envelope
with the following fields:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| `kind` | `run_requeue` (constant `RunRequeueEventKind`) |
| `summary` | `run requeued` |
| `actor` | Operator identity (`unknown`/`anonymous` when no identity is resolvable from the inbound HTTP request) |
| `source` | `graphql:runRequeue` |
| `body` | Single line: `identity=<kind> actor=<actor> run_id=<runId> idempotency_key=<key> layer_override=<layer-or-empty>` |

The `body` line is structured for grep/jq parsing: each token is a
`key=value` pair separated by single spaces. `layer_override` is the
empty string when the caller did not pass `RunRequeueInput.layer`.
Operator identity is captured via the same audit pathway used by
operator-prompt beads (ADR-021); when the request carries no
identifying header, the audit fields fall back to
`identity=unknown actor=anonymous`.

Consumers of the audit log (FEAT-008 Runs row expansion, evaluation
skills under FEAT-019) treat `run_requeue` events as the canonical
record that a re-queue happened: there is no separate persisted
`requeue` record beyond (a) the new `open` status on the originating
bead and (b) this event entry.

### Layer-to-substrate mapping for the Runs UI

The web Runs view (FEAT-008 §5, FEAT-021) renders three layer chips
backed by the unified substrate plus the legacy detail backings:

| Chip | Substrate row | Detail backing for row expansion |
|---|---|---|
| `work` | `layer: 3` Run record | Layer-3 record's queue inputs / stop-condition log / child layer-2 attempt ids |
| `try` | `layer: 2` Run record | The `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundle attached to the layer-2 record (manifest, prompt, result, checks, verdict) |
| `run` | `layer: 1` Run record | The associated `AgentSession` row (prompt / response / stderr / billing / cached-token detail) joined onto the layer-1 record |

`AgentSession` rows that have no parent layer-2 attempt (raw `ddx
agent log` invocations) surface as synthesized `layer=run` Runs rows
keyed by session id, so no agent-session row is dropped during the
Sessions/Executions tab retirement.

input.new_string:
## CONTRACT-003 Amendment

This feature requires two narrow read-only-amendment-adjacent changes
to CONTRACT-003: write surfaces for `artifactRegenerate` (HTTP/MCP) and
`runRequeue` (GraphQL). `runRequeue` does not invoke an agent — it
reopens the originating bead so the existing `ddx agent execute-loop`
can claim it again — but it is still a write because it mutates bead
state and appends an audit event (see "Re-queue audit events" above).
No other write surfaces are added; in particular, layer-1, layer-2,
and layer-3 invocation remain CLI-only.

### Project Navigation Tabs

Each `/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/...` page exposes the following
project-scoped tabs in the global navigation, in this order:

| Tab | Route segment | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Overview | (none) | Project home, recent activity, operator-prompt input (ADR-021) |
| Beads | `beads` | Bead list / kanban / ready / detail (US-082*) |
| Artifacts | `artifacts` | Artifact browser (US-080, US-081b, US-083) |
| Graph | `graph` | Document dependency graph (US-081, US-081c) |
| Runs | `runs` | Layer-aware run history with `work` / `try` / `run` chips, row expansion, and re-queue (US-086b, US-086c) |
| Workers | `workers` | Live + recent worker processes for this project (US-085b, US-086, US-086a) |
| Personas | `personas` | Persona browser + role bindings (US-097) |
| Plugins | `plugins` | Plugin registry + install/uninstall (US-098) |
| Commits | `commits` | Project git commit log (US-093, FEAT-021) |
| Efficacy | `efficacy` | Model efficacy + comparisons (US-096) |

**Sessions and Executions are retired as top-level tabs** (Story 8). The
unified Runs tab carries layer chips and inline row expansion that
surface what those tabs used to show:

- `layer=run` rows expand to AgentSession transcript / billing / cached
  tokens / stderr (the old Sessions detail).
- `layer=try` rows expand to the `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundle
  metadata, check results, and verdict (the old Executions detail).
- `layer=work` rows expand to queue inputs and child run links.

**URL deprecation policy.** During the deprecation window, the legacy
URLs respond with **302 redirects (NOT 301)** to the matching filtered
Runs URL with all query params preserved, plus a `Sunset` response
header that names the cutoff date for the redirects:

| Legacy URL | Redirect target |
|---|---|
| `/sessions` | `/runs?layer=run` |
| `/sessions#<id>` | `/runs?layer=run&session=<id>` |
| `/executions[?bead=&verdict=&harness=&q=&after=]` | `/runs?layer=try` with the original query string preserved verbatim |
| `/executions/[id]` | `/runs/[runId]?bundle=true` |

302 (not 301) is intentional: bookmarks and external link aggregators
should not cache the redirect permanently while we still own the legacy
routes. The redirect response carries `Sunset: <RFC 9745 date>` and the
project nav drops the Sessions/Executions entries once the redirects and
the internal-link sweep land (`shellRoutes.ts`, `BeadDetail.svelte`,
commits page, agent page, Workers detail).

     column sort on Started / Duration / Cost. Search/sort/filter primitives
     are the shared Story 6 chip + sort-header + search-input components
     (PRIMITIVE reuse — Runs has its own page model for live updates,
     parent/child hierarchy, layer expansion, and re-queue affordances).
     Story 7's virtualization wrapper is reused with a Runs-specific live
     adapter when row counts exceed 1k.
   - Inline row expansion is **layer-aware** (replaces the retired Sessions
     and Executions top-level tabs):
     - `layer=work` row expands to queue inputs, selected beads, stop
       condition, retry/defer decisions, and child try-attempt links in
       chronological order.
     - `layer=try` row expands to bead id, base/result revisions, isolated
       worktree path, merge/preserve outcome, checks, verdict, and child
       layer-1 run links — sourced losslessly from the
       `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundle attached to the layer-2 record.
     - `layer=run` row expands to prompt/config summary, power bounds,
       selected harness/provider/model, token + cached-token + cost +
       duration signals, output, stderr, and evidence links — sourced
       losslessly from the joined `AgentSession` row (FEAT-010 §"Layer-to-
       substrate mapping for the Runs UI").
   - Drill-down links preserve hierarchy in both directions:
     `work` -> `try` -> `run`, with breadcrumbs back to parent records
   - Artifact-producing runs show `produces_artifact`; artifact pages link back
     to the producing run and expose regeneration history
   - **Re-queue affordances** (Story 8):
     - `layer=try` rows show a `Re-queue` button when the originating bead is
       open AND no try is currently active for that bead. The button opens a
       confirmation dialog prefilled with the original config; the client
       generates the `idempotencyKey` and sends it with the
       `runRequeue` mutation (FEAT-010).
     - `layer=run` rows show `Re-queue` only when prompt / harness / model are
       replayable; re-queueing a successful run requires double-confirmation.
     - `layer=work` rows show **`Start worker from this drain`** (different
       label from "Re-queue"; same dispatch path) prefilled with the
       original queue inputs, stop condition, selected beads, and harness /
       profile from the original work run.
     - Re-queue against a closed bead is disabled and surfaces a
       "Reopen bead first" link.
     - Every successful re-queue emits a `run_requeue` audit event on the
       originating bead (schema: FEAT-010 §"Re-queue audit events").
   - Layer-aware run views consume read-only GraphQL/HTTP state except for the
     narrow `artifactRegenerate` and `runRequeue` mutations.

### US-081c: Developer Repairs Graph Integrity Issues from the UI
input.new_string:
**E2E Test:** `runs.spec.ts` — full workflow: navigate to project runs → filter by layer `work` → click work record → verify fields and child tries → click try → verify fields and child runs → click layer-1 run → verify all fields → follow evidence link → press Back through breadcrumbs to run list → verify filter state restored

### US-086c: Operator Re-queues a Run from the Runs Tab
**As an** operator who wants fresh evidence on an existing bead or to
  restart a queue drain
**I want** to re-queue a `try` or `run` row, and to start a new worker
  from a `work` row, directly from the Runs tab
**So that** I do not have to leave the UI to dispatch follow-up work

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Given I am on a `layer=try` row whose originating bead is open and has
  no active try, then a `Re-queue` button is visible. Clicking it opens a
  confirmation dialog prefilled with the original `(bead, harness, model)`
  config; the client generates an `idempotencyKey` and submits it with the
  `runRequeue` mutation (FEAT-010); on success the originating bead is
  reopened and a `run_requeue` audit event appears on the bead
- Given I am on a `layer=run` row with replayable `(prompt, harness,
  model)`, then `Re-queue` is visible. If the run terminated with
  success, the dialog requires explicit double-confirmation
- Given I am on a `layer=work` row, then a **`Start worker from this
  drain`** action is visible (instead of `Re-queue`). Confirming it
  dispatches a new worker prefilled with the original queue inputs, stop
  condition, selected beads, and harness/profile
- Given the originating bead is closed, then the `Re-queue` button is
  disabled and a "Reopen bead first" link is shown in its place
- Given a duplicate submission (double-click race or accidental retry)
  arrives with the same `idempotencyKey`, then the second response
  carries `deduplicated=true`, the bead is reopened only once, and only
  one `run_requeue` audit event is appended

**E2E Test:** `runs.spec.ts` — full workflow: open Runs tab → click try-row Re-queue → verify confirmation dialog with prefilled config → confirm → verify originating bead reopens and audit event appears → re-click Re-queue (race) → verify single audit event and `deduplicated=true` response → switch to closed-bead row → verify Re-queue disabled with "Reopen bead first" link → switch to layer=work row → verify `Start worker from this drain` action prefills queue inputs → confirm → verify new worker appears in Workers tab
